name: Suspected anaphylaxis after medicine exposure
risk: Rapid-onset airway/breathing/circulation compromise after a medicine exposure is anaphylaxis until proven otherwise.
safety_action: Emergency escalation immediately. First-line treatment is IM adrenaline/epinephrine according to local anaphylaxis protocol, with airway/oxygen, IV fluids, monitoring, and documentation of the suspected allergy.
source: Local Emergency Rule: suspected medicine-induced anaphylaxis v1

name: ACE inhibitor/ARB potassium and renal monitoring
risk: Hyperkalemia and renal function deterioration can be missed if K+ and renal function are unknown.
safety_action: Do not reassure that potassium supplementation is safe with ACEI/ARB unless labs and indication are known.
source: Local Safety Rule: ACEI/ARB monitoring v1

name: NSAID renal-risk screen
risk: NSAIDs can precipitate AKI and worsen blood pressure/heart failure in susceptible patients.
safety_action: Prefer non-NSAID options or short supervised use in renal/cardiovascular high-risk patients.
source: Local Safety Rule: NSAID renal screen v1

name: Warfarin bleeding-risk screen
risk: Warfarin has high harm potential when interacting drugs or bleeding symptoms are present.
safety_action: Bleeding symptoms or very high INR require urgent clinician assessment; do not advise self-adjustment without prescriber guidance.
source: Local Safety Rule: warfarin bleeding screen v1

name: Insulin hypoglycemia and dose stacking screen
risk: Insulin dose errors and dose stacking can cause severe hypoglycemia.
safety_action: If severe hypoglycemia, unconsciousness, seizure, or inability to take oral glucose occurs, urgent emergency care is required.
source: Local Safety Rule: insulin hypoglycemia v1

name: Pregnancy red-flag screen
risk: Pregnancy questions may involve maternal/fetal safety and need conservative triage.
safety_action: Reduced fetal movement, bleeding, severe abdominal pain, severe headache/visual symptoms, or severe hypertension need urgent obstetric assessment.
source: Local Safety Rule: pregnancy red flags v1

name: SGLT2 inhibitor sick-day / acute illness screen
risk: During acute illness, dehydration, hypotension, AKI, fasting, or acidosis, SGLT2 inhibitors can worsen volume depletion/AKI and increase euglycemic ketoacidosis risk.
required_info: serum or urine ketones if unwell/acidosis
safety_action: Flag for urgent clinician review and hold during acute illness/dehydration/AKI/acidosis. Restart only after acute illness resolves, patient is eating/drinking normally, euvolemic, hemodynamically stable, acidosis/ketosis excluded or resolved, and renal function is stable; indication and eGFR threshold should follow local label/guideline.
source: Local Safety Rule: SGLT2 sick-day / acute illness v2

name: Colchicine toxicity with CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors
risk: Colchicine exposure can rise markedly with strong CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors such as clarithromycin, especially with renal or hepatic impairment, causing severe GI toxicity, myopathy/rhabdomyolysis, cytopenias, and multi-organ toxicity.
safety_action: Treat colchicine plus strong CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitor exposure in renal/hepatic impairment as high-risk; urgently flag for prescriber review to stop/avoid the combination and choose alternatives.
source: Local Safety Rule: colchicine + strong CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitor v1

name: Serotonin syndrome recognition and escalation
trigger_drug_classes: opioid analgesic with serotonin/norepinephrine reuptake inhibition
risk: Altered mental status, autonomic instability, and neuromuscular findings after serotonergic drug exposure can indicate serotonin syndrome.
safety_action: Urgent ED/prescriber escalation. Flag serotonergic medicines for immediate clinician review; supportive care, benzodiazepines, cooling, and cyproheptadine are clinician-managed.
source: Local Emergency Rule: serotonin syndrome recognition v1

name: Drug-induced QT prolongation risk
risk: QTc >500 ms or QT prolongation plus low potassium/magnesium increases torsades de pointes risk.
safety_action: Flag for urgent clinician review, telemetry, correction of K/Mg, and avoidance/holding of QT-prolonging contributors according to local protocol.
source: Local Safety Rule: QT prolongation and torsades prevention v1

name: SSRI/SNRI/thiazide hyponatremia screen
risk: SSRIs/SNRIs and thiazides can cause or worsen hyponatremia, especially in older adults; confusion, falls, or seizures increase urgency.
safety_action: Flag for prescriber review of serotonergic antidepressants and thiazide; sodium correction must be monitored carefully to avoid overcorrection.
source: Local Safety Rule: antidepressant/thiazide hyponatremia v1

name: DOAC fall and hemoglobin-drop screen
trigger_drug_classes: direct oral factor Xa inhibitor anticoagulant
risk: A recent fall or trauma while on a DOAC plus confusion or hemoglobin drop should be treated as possible occult bleeding until assessed.
safety_action: Urgently flag for clinician assessment for occult bleeding; reversal/holding decisions are clinician- and protocol-directed based on bleeding severity and thromboembolic risk.
source: Local Safety Rule: DOAC fall and occult bleeding v1
